coredata.dc:description: In this work, a green and uncomplicated solvothermal method is employed to synthesize multicolor carbon dots (CDs), utilizing Mimosa pudica (M.P) leaves as the exclusive carbon source. CDs synthesized exhibited well-defined, spherical morphology with smooth surfaces and average diameters of ∼6.7 nm (RCDs) and ∼6.5 nm (BCDs), as confirmed by SEM and TEM. HR-TEM revealed a lattice spacing of 0.21 and 0.23 nm, attributed to the (100) plane of RCDs and BCDs. Raman and XRD studies showed increased graphitization and crystallinity with rising synthesis temperatures. The ID/IG ratio decreased from 1.05 (RCDs) to 0.92 (BCDs), indicating higher graphitic order in BCDs. BCDs showed a longer fluorescence lifetime (4.82 ns) compared to RCDs (2.15 ns), with RCDs having a higher quantum yield (15.63 %) due to chlorophyll-based emissions. A novel sandwich-type R/B-CDs@PVA composite film is fabricated by integrating RCDs into PVA and BCDs into a cellulose acetate (CA) top layer, using a dip-coating method. The composite films exhibited high tensile strength (∼76.85 MPa), with minor compromise in elongation due to the rigid CA coating, retaining good flexibility. Indoor cultivation trials with tomato plants demonstrated significant improvements in plant height, chlorophyll content, biomass, and leaf area under R/B-CDs-based LEDs compared to sunlight or light conversion films (LCF) exposure. Sensitive and selective detection of Resveratrol (Res) is achieved using BCDs through fluorescence quenching, showing a low detection limit (LOD = 7.76 mg/mL), with negligible interference from other substances. This study successfully demonstrates the multifunctional application of R/B CDs@PVA composite films in plant-growth LEDs, LCF and sensitive Res detection. The tailored spectral output significantly enhanced photosynthesis and biomass in tomato plants, while BCDs enabled ultrasensitive biosensing through dual-mode fluorescence quenching.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a green and uncomplicated solvothermal method is employed to synthesize multicolor carbon dots (CDs), utilizing Mimosa pudica (M.P) leaves as the exclusive carbon source. CDs synthesized exhibited well-defined, spherical morphology with smooth surfaces and average diameters of ∼6.7 nm (RCDs) and ∼6.5 nm (BCDs), as confirmed by SEM and TEM. HR-TEM revealed a lattice spacing of 0.21 and 0.23 nm, attributed to the (100) plane of RCDs and BCDs. Raman and XRD studies showed increased graphitization and crystallinity with rising synthesis temperatures. The ID/IG ratio decreased from 1.05 (RCDs) to 0.92 (BCDs), indicating higher graphitic order in BCDs. BCDs showed a longer fluorescence lifetime (4.82 ns) compared to RCDs (2.15 ns), with RCDs having a higher quantum yield (15.63 %) due to chlorophyll-based emissions. A novel sandwich-type R/B-CDs@PVA composite film is fabricated by integrating RCDs into PVA and BCDs into a cellulose acetate (CA) top layer, using a dip-coating method. The composite films exhibited high tensile strength (∼76.85 MPa), with minor compromise in elongation due to the rigid CA coating, retaining good flexibility. Indoor cultivation trials with tomato plants demonstrated significant improvements in plant height, chlorophyll content, biomass, and leaf area under R/B-CDs-based LEDs compared to sunlight or light conversion films (LCF) exposure. Sensitive and selective detection of Resveratrol (Res) is achieved using BCDs through fluorescence quenching, showing a low detection limit (LOD = 7.76 mg/mL), with negligible interference from other substances. This study successfully demonstrates the multifunctional application of R/B CDs@PVA composite films in plant-growth LEDs, LCF and sensitive Res detection. The tailored spectral output significantly enhanced photosynthesis and biomass in tomato plants, while BCDs enabled ultrasensitive biosensing through dual-mode fluorescence quenching.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Biomass-derived CDs have emerged as a new class of fluorescent nanomaterials due to their tunable luminescence, low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, and environmental sustainability [1,2] . Unlike traditional quantum dots and organic dyes [3], CDs offer unique advantages that have expanded their applications into biosensing [4], optical imaging [5], and diagnostics [6]. Although multicolour CDs have been synthesized using small organic molecules such as citric acid, phenylenediamine, or dihydroxybenzene [7], these methods often involve hazardous chemicals and higher energy consumption [8]. As a result, there is growing interest in developing green and cost-effective methods for producing multicolour CDs from sustainable biomass sources.

However, biomass-based CDs present challenges due to the complex composition of natural precursors, which hinders a clear understanding of their structure-luminescence relationship. Furthermore, the ability to produce multicolour emissions from a single biomass source remains rare, limiting large-scale feasibility. Therefore, advancing simple, environmentally friendly methods for synthesizing multicolour CDs with tunable optical properties from a single biomass remains a key research priority.

In recent developments, CDs have been explored for light-emitting diodes (LEDs), light-converting films (LCFs), sensors, and bioimaging due to their strong photoluminescence, eco-friendliness, and low production costs [9–11]. Among these, enhancing solar energy use in agriculture via down-conversion of harmful ultraviolet (UV) light into photosynthetically active radiation (PAR) is gaining traction [12]. Blue (400–500 nm) and red (590–700 nm) light are particularly critical for plant growth, while UV light especially UV-B and UV-C can hinder development and damage plant DNA [13–15]. Converting UV into blue/red light thus holds promise for improving photosynthetic efficiency and plant productivity in greenhouses.

Though root uptake of CDs in plants like lettuce, maize, and mung beans has shown safe profiles [16,17], limited research exists on integrating CD embedded films in light management systems directly interacting with plant foliage. Moreover, while rare-earth elements have been used in light-converting materials, they suffer from high cost and limited tunability [18–20]. In contrast, CDs offer cost-effective, tunable emission spectra [21–24]. For example, Wang et al. demonstrated multicolour white LEDs using CDs combined with UV chips [25].

In the context of agriculture, LCFs composed of polymer matrices embedded with photoluminescent agents have improved light use efficiency [26]. However, traditional materials like rare-earth complexes or organic dyes face limitations in spectral alignment, stability, and sustainability [27,28]. CDs present a compelling alternative due to their excellent optical stability and low environmental impact [29]. Notably, Li et al. [30] developed red/blue dual-emissive CDs from Phellodendron chinense that enhanced photosynthesis by matching plant pigment absorption spectra, while Ge et al. [31] tuned CD emission colors using furfural derivatives for Arabidopsis plant growth. Despite these advancements, many LCFs rely on fossil-based polymers like polyethylene, raising ecological concerns [32]. Efforts to replace them with biodegradable matrices such as PVA or cellulose acetate have shown promise [33–35], though challenges like water resistance under high humidity persist [36].

Simultaneously, CDs have shown strong potential in fluorescence-based sensing. Resveratrol (Res), a natural polyphenol with antioxidant and therapeutic properties, requires accurate quantification due to its potential toxicity at high doses [37–39]. Standard analytical techniques (HPLC, GC-MS, CE) [40–43] are often time-consuming and costly, making fluorescent sensing via CDs an attractive alternative due to its simplicity, speed, and environmental friendliness. Chenzhao Wang et al. [44] reported the fluorescence-based detection of resveratrol (Res) using banana peel-derived nitrogen-doped carbon dots (BP-N-CDs), achieving excellent sensitivity with a limit of detection (LOD) of 2.21 ng/mL. A decrease in fluorescence intensity is observed with increasing Res concentrations (9.7 × 10⁻⁶ to 0.1 mg/mL), showing a linear relationship with a regression equation of Y= 6753,394x+ 1194,428 and R2= 0.9997. The LOD is calculated using the formula LOD= 3σ/k. Similarly, D. Hana et al. [45] investigated trans-resveratrol (t-Res) detection using carbon dots (CDs). Standard t-Res solutions (10–200 μM) are prepared in ethanol and stored at –4 °C in the dark. After mixing with CDs, fluorescence spectra are recorded at 460 nm following 5 min of incubation. A consistent decrease in fluorescence intensity with increasing t-Res concentration is observed, with a linear correlation (R2=0.9993) and an LOD of 3 μM. In the present study, a comparable fluorescence quenching effect is observed with increasing Res concentrations (0–10 mg/mL). A strong linear correlation is established (R2=0.99), and the LOD is determined to be 7.76 ng/mL, calculated using LOD= 3σ/k, validating the sensitivity and reliability of the developed detection method. The present study demonstrates a fluorescence detection method with a broad dynamic range (0–10 mg/mL) and a low LOD. Although the LOD is slightly higher than that of Wang et al., our method significantly expands the linear detection range, which is advantageous for applications requiring quantification over a wide concentration span, such as in food and nutraceutical matrices. Furthermore, the high correlation coefficient (R2=0.99 R) confirms the reliability and reproducibility of our method.

In this study, a green solvothermal method is used to synthesize RCDs and BCDs from M.P leaves as the sole carbon source. A sandwich-type composite film (R/B-CDs@PVA) is fabricated by incorporating RCDs in a PVA layer and BCDs in a CA top layer via dip-coating. The composite exhibited strong mechanical strength (∼76.85 MPa) and retained flexibility despite the rigid CA coating. It also showed good transparency and multicolour emission under UV light, tunable by BCD concentration. In indoor trials, tomato plants grown under R/B-CDs-based LEDs showed notable improvements in height, leaf area, biomass, and chlorophyll content versus controls (sunlight or traditional LCFs). These results highlight the film's dual function as an efficient light-converting material and plant-growth enhancer. Overall, this work offers a green, economical route to multicolour CDs from biomass, with promising applications in sustainable agriculture and environmental biosensing.

M.P leaves are collected from the campus of Tumkur University, Karnataka, India on August 10th 2024. Deionized water with a resistivity of 18.1 MΩ.cm is procured using a Milli-Q water purification system. The other chemicals used in this study include anhydrous ethanol (AR grade), analytical-grade polyvinyl alcohol (PVA 1788), cellulose acetate, methylene chloride, N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), and Resveratrol (Res). Additional compounds used are anthocyanins (Ant), Kaempferol (Kae), gallic acid (GA), rutin (Rut), chlorogenic acid, pterostilbene (Pte), cysteine (Cys), glutamic acid (Glu), histidine (His), vitamin A (VA), vitamin C (VC), glucose (Glu), maltose (Mal), aluminium hydroxide (Al(OH)3·3H2O), potassium chloride (KCl), calcium chloride (CaCl2), manganese chloride tetrahydrate (MnCl2·4H2O), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), sodium chloride (NaCl), zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O), Ferric chloride (FeCl3), Ferrous sulphate (FeSO4), potassium carbonate (K2CO3), diuron (Diu), monuron (Mon), soy isoflavones (SI), and piceatannol (Pic). All these reagents are sourced from Sigma-Aldrich and are used without any additional purification. Bananas, grapes, blueberries, and peanuts required for analysis are purchased from a nearby supermarket.

Fresh M.P leaves are thoroughly washed to remove any soil or dust and dried in an oven at 60 °C for 2 h. While still warm, the dried leaves are ground into a fine powder. Approximately 0.1 g of this leaf powder is dispersed in 30 mL of anhydrous ethanol and transferred into a 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene-lined autoclave. The solvothermal process is conducted at 150 °C for 4 h in a (Teflon)-lined autoclave reactor to obtain RCDs. In a similar process, 0.10 g of crushed moss powder is mixed with 30 mL of anhydrous ethanol and subjected to solvothermal treatment at 220 °C for 16 h to yield BCDs. After the reaction, the autoclaves are cooled to room temperature. The resultant products are purified using silica gel column chromatography with ethanol as the mobile phase. Solvent removal is performed using rotary evaporation, followed by vacuum drying at 80 °C for 48 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs with varying temperature and time
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the influence of temperature and duration on CD fluorescence, 0.1 g of M.P leaf powder is dispersed in 30 mL of anhydrous ethanol and placed in a 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene-lined autoclave. The solvothermal reaction is carried out at different temperatures and time durations, as illustrated in Fig. 1 a. post-reaction, all the samples are subjected to identical purification and drying procedures. Notably, CDs synthesized at 150 °C for 4 h exhibited strong red fluorescence, whereas, those produced at 220 °C for 16 h showed prominent blue fluorescence. These two samples are chosen for detailed examination in the subsequent analyses.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of R/B(5 wt%)-CDs@PVA composite films
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of R/B(5 wt%)-CDs@PVA composite films is executed in two distinct steps. Initially, 100 mg of RCDs powder is dispersed in 50 mL of ethanol to produce a red-fluorescent solution. Separately, 5 g of PVA is dissolved in distilled water at 90 °C using an oil bath. The RCDs solution is then added to the PVA solution while maintaining the temperature at 90 °C, resulting in a uniform RCDs/PVA mixture (RCDs concentration: 1 wt%). After cooling the solution to ambient temperature, 500 μg of ascorbic acid (AA) is added. The resulting RCDs/PVA mixture is allowed to dry naturally in air to form a composite film.

In the second stage, 100 mg of BCDs powder is dissolved in 50 mL of DMF to obtain a blue-emitting solution. Subsequently, 80 g of CA is dissolved in 400 mL of tetrahydrofuran (THF) to prepare a polymer matrix. Varying quantities of the BCDs solution are then added and stirred into the CA/THF matrix to obtain BCDs/CA solutions with BCDs content at 1, 5, and 10 wt%. The resulting BCDs/CA solution is used as a coating medium, and the RCDs/PVA films are immersed into this solution at a constant rate of 40 mm/min using a dip-coating setup. After a 5-second immersion period, the films are withdrawn at 5 mm/min. The coated films are air-dried at room temperature for 2 min, followed by water soaking for 10 min, and finally dried again under ambient conditions for 2 min to produce the R/B(5 wt%)-CDs@PVA composite films. A schematic depiction of the film fabrication process is presented in Fig. 1 b.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water contact angle (WCA) and mechanical property measurements
ce:section.ce:para: The WCA of the composite films is assessed following established protocols [46], and the contact angle measurements are conducted using ImageJ software. The R/B-CDs@PVA nanocomposite films are fixed onto a smooth metallic base, and droplets (4 μL) of deionized water, fruit juice, milk, and castor oil are carefully placed on the film surface. After 1 min, the contact angles are measured and recorded. The mechanical characteristics of the films, including tensile strength (Ts), Young’s modulus (Ym), and elongation at break (Eb), are evaluated using a universal testing machine (UTM, Lloyd Instruments, model 7200). The tests adhered to ASTM D 882–9156 standards and are conducted at room temperature under air with a crosshead speed of 5 mm/min. Film specimens are cut to dimensions of 10 cm in length and 2.5 cm in width before testing.

The R/B-CDs@PVA composite films are utilized as luminescent layers for constructing LED devices. These composite films are precisely aligned and mounted onto 395 nm LED chips to evaluate their electroluminescent performance. A regulated power source is connected to serve as the LED driver, ensuring consistent operation. The electroluminescence spectra of the fabricated LED units are subsequently recorded to assess their light conversion capabilities and determine their applicability for plant growth enhancement.

Tomato seedlings, approximately 10 days old, are procured locally and transferred into plastic pots (dimensions: 10 × 7 × 8.5 cm) containing a peat-based growth medium. A controlled growth experiment is conducted under three lighting conditions as described below:
Control Group (Sunlight): Seedlings are exposed to natural sunlight for 8–12 h per day.

LCFs Group: Plants are grown for 10 days at 28 °C under an illumination level of 2700 lux, maintaining an 18 h light and 6 h dark cycle. R/B(5 wt%)-CDs@PVA composite films are positioned 20 cm above the plants to facilitate spectral light conversion.

LED Group: Plants are cultivated under lighting provided by LEDs fabricated using R/B(5 wt%)-CDs@PVA composite films. The environmental conditions are kept identical to those in the LCFs group, with the LED source maintained at a fixed distance of 20 cm above the plants.

Throughout the cultivation period, all tomato plants are watered daily and received identical applications of water-soluble fertilizer to ensure consistent nutrient availability across all experimental groups.

Chlorophyll concentration is determined following the procedure established by Harborne [47]. Fresh tomato leaves (∼ 0.1 g) are chopped and ground in 10 mL of 80 % acetone solution. The resulting mixture is centrifuged at 5000–10,000 rpm for 5 min, and the clear supernatant is collected. The extraction process is repeated until the residue appeared colorless. Absorbance readings of the collected extract are recorded at 645 nm and 663 nm using acetone as the blank. The concentrations of chlorophyll a, chlorophyll b, and total chlorophyll are calculated using Arnon’s equations [48]:
(1) Chlorophyll a (mg/mL) = (12.7 × A663 – 2.69 × A645)
(2) Chlorophyll b (mg/mL) = (22.9 × A645 – 4.68 × A663)
(3) Total Chlorophyll (mg/mL) = (20.2 × A645 + 8.02 × A663)

Here, A663 and A645 correspond to the absorbance at 663 nm and 645 nm, respectively. Chla and Chlb refer to chlorophyll a and chlorophyll b concentrations, expressed in mg/g, with V denoting the volume of the extract (mL), and m representing the fresh leaf weight (g).

To assess sensitivity, 200 µL of BCDs solution is mixed with 2.8 mL of Res standard solutions of varying concentrations, followed by shaking for 10 seconds. The fluorescence spectra are measured at the optimal excitation wavelength. A calibration curve is established by plotting fluorescence intensity against Res concentration, from which the method's sensitivity is determined. Selectivity is evaluated by applying the same protocol to a series of potential interfering substances, including Al3+, Ca2+, Cu2+, K+, Zn2+, Na+, Fe2+, Mg2+, F-, Cys, Glu, His, Mal, Arg, VA, VC, Mon, and Diu, to examine the specificity of the BCDs in detecting Res.

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns are recorded in the 2θ range of 10°–80° using a Shimadzu 7000 diffractometer, equipped with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.541 Å), operating at 36 kV and 30 mA. The scan rate and step size are set to 2°/min and 0.02°, respectively. The morphology and particle size of the synthesized CDs are observed via transmission electron microscopy (TEM) using a JEM-2010 instrument at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis is carried out on a VG ESCALAB MKII spectrometer, fitted with an aluminium Kα X-ray source and an XR4 dual anode system, to determine surface elemental composition and bonding states. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) is performed using a Nicolet FTIR-400 instrument, with KBr pellet samples, to analyse the functional groups present in the CDs. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra are acquired using a Horiba Jobin Yvon spectrofluorometer, and fluorescence lifetime decay curves are generated with a QM8000 FL spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon Inc., Edison, NJ, USA). The quantum yield (QY) of the CDs is measured with a C9920–02G quantum efficiency measurement system. Raman spectroscopy is conducted using a Horiba Scientific LabRAM HR Evolution spectrometer, employing a 532 nm laser as the excitation source. Time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) measurements are performed using a FluoroLog 3 system to record PL lifetimes. Elemental composition and distribution are analyzed through energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). Thermal properties including weight loss, transition temperature (Tg), and melting temperature (Tm) of the composite films are studied using a SDT Q600 V20.9 simultaneous thermal analyzer (Shimadzu), with a heating rate of 10 °C/min under nitrogen flow (100 mL/min) up to 700 °C. EL spectra and the chromaticity coordinates (CIE-1931) of the remote phosphor-based LEDs are evaluated using a LABSPHERE light measurement system comprising a fiber-optic spectrometer and an integrating sphere.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs derived from M. P leaves
ce:section.ce:para:
To understand the morphological characteristics and structural features of the synthesized CDs, scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) analyses are conducted. Figs. 2 a and 2 b display the SEM images of RCDs and BCDs, respectively, revealing a well-defined spherical morphology with smooth surfaces in both cases. The TEM images provided in Figs. 2 c and 2 d confirm the spherical shape of the CDs. The particle size distribution histograms (Figures 2c
1
and 2d
1 ) indicate that the average diameters of RCDs and BCDs are approximately 6.71 nm and 6.46 nm, respectively. HRTEM images further illustrate the presence of distinct lattice fringes in both RCDs and BCDs with an interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon, implying a graphite-like nanostructure (Figs. 2 e and 2 f) [49]. Elemental analysis using energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) confirmed the presence of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (O) in both samples (Fig. 2 g and 2h). A notable observation is the lower nitrogen content in BCDs compared to RCDs. Raman spectroscopy provided insights into the structural order of the CDs. The intensity ratio of the D band (1370 cm−1) to the G band (1638 cm−1), denoted as ID/IG, is found to be 1.05 for RCDs and 0.92 for BCDs (Fig. 2 i). This ratio serves as an indicator of the degree of graphitization and the proportion of sp3 to sp2 hybridized carbon [50]. The decreasing trend in ID/IG values suggests that higher synthesis temperatures enhance the graphitic nature of the material. Elevated temperatures likely induce the degradation of chlorophyll characterized by a porphyrin structure resulting in a transition toward carbon core formation and greater structural graphitization [51].

Surface functional groups are investigated via Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, with results shown in Fig. 2 j. Both RCDs and BCDs displayed similar spectral profiles. The FT-IR spectra revealed key characteristic bands corresponding to N–H/O–H stretching (∼3320 cm−1), CO stretching (∼1609 cm−1), CN (∼1468 cm−1), CC (∼1363 cm−1), C–N (∼1098 cm−1), and C–O (∼997 cm−1), confirming the aromatic nature of the CDs [52]. Notably, the CN peak intensity in BCDs is significantly reduced compared to RCDs, which could be attributed to the thermal breakdown of the porphyrin ring, contributing to the formation of carbon cores. This observation is consistent with the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) results. To gain deeper insights into the elemental composition and chemical states, XPS analyses are carried out. The survey spectra (Figure S1a) showed that both RCDs and BCDs primarily contain C, N, and O. High-resolution C 1 s spectra revealed three distinct peaks, assigned to C–C/CC (∼284.2 eV), C–N/C–O (∼285.7 eV), and CN/CO (∼287.1 eV) (Figure S1b). The O 1 s spectra (Figure S1c) displayed two peaks located at ∼531.6 eV and ∼532.4 eV, corresponding to CO and C–O functional groups, respectively [53]. Deconvolution of the N 1 s spectra (Figure S1d) yielded two peaks at ∼399.2 eV and ∼399.9 eV, corresponding to pyrrolic and graphitic nitrogen species [54]. Interestingly, the relative intensity of the C–C/CC component decreased from 73.26 % in RCDs to 66.38 % in BCDs (Figure S1b), reflecting the changes in fluorescence emission centers as a function of reaction temperature, consistent with Raman observations. XRD patterns (Figs. 2 k and 2 l) further supported the increased graphitization at higher synthesis temperatures. The RCDs exhibited a broad (002) diffraction peak at approximately 19.8°, while the corresponding peak in BCDs is sharper and appeared at 21.2°. Additionally, minor peaks around 40.6° for RCDs and 41.1° for BCDs are observed, attributed to the (100) planes. The sharper peak in BCDs suggests better crystallinity and graphitic ordering, corroborating the Raman results [55].

The optical behavior of the CDs is studied to understand their fluorescence characteristics under different reaction conditions. Since the emission properties are hypothesized to be influenced by chlorophyll-related moieties, systematic changes in temperature and time are applied to induce a shift from defect-driven red emission to carbon-core-based blue fluorescence. The use of M.P leaves as precursors provided a promising platform for tuning emission characteristics, successfully achieving a range from red to near-white and finally to blue fluorescence. The fluorescence spectra ( Figs. 3 a and 3 b) demonstrated a clear and consistent shift in emission based on synthesis conditions. Typical absorption peaks in the 200–250 nm range are observed, corresponding to π–π* transitions in aromatic structures. RCDs displayed distinct absorption features near 400–460 nm and a minor peak between 600–670 nm, typically assigned to the Soret and Q bands of chlorophyll-derived porphyrins, respectively (Fig. 3 a) [56]. When excited at 655 nm, the emission peak aligned with the absorption peak at ∼480 nm, suggesting chlorophyll-based emission in RCDs. Emission spectra under different excitation wavelengths (Figure S2a) indicated that RCDs exhibited excitation-independent fluorescence. As reaction temperature increased to 220 °C, the chlorophyll absorption features disappeared (Fig. 3 b), implying thermal decomposition of chlorophyll structures. At an excitation wavelength of 380 nm, BCDs exhibited blue emission at 480 nm. This behaviour indicates a gradual shift of the fluorescence centre from chlorophyll to the carbon core with increasing temperature. Emission spectra of BCDs under various excitations (300–420 nm, Figure S2b) revealed that the emission peak position remained largely unchanged, suggesting excitation-independent behaviour, differing from previously reported BCDs [57].

Fluorescence lifetime analysis (Fig. 3 c) further elucidated this transition. RCDs showed a shorter average lifetime of 2.15 ns, while BCDs exhibited a longer lifetime of 4.82 ns, indicating a dominance of more stable carbon core emission in BCDs. This observation aligns with the findings of F. Qin et al. [58], who reported a red-to-blue fluorescence transition in moss-based ethanol extracts with rising temperatures, attributing red emission to chlorophyll residues and blue emission to the carbon core. Quantum yield (QY) measurements showed that RCDs had a higher QY (15.63 %) compared to BCDs (12.23 %) (Figs. 3 d and 3 e). This is primarily due to the chlorophyll-related structures contributing to stronger red fluorescence in RCDs. As temperature increases, these structures degrade, reducing defect-based emission and shifting fluorescence to less efficient carbon core centres, thereby lowering QY despite better structural ordering. Thus, the higher QY in RCDs arises from synergistic emission from both chlorophyll and carbon core states, whereas BCDs rely solely on core-based emission. Stability tests over 150 days indicated a significant decline in fluorescence intensity for RCDs, likely due to the degradation of chlorophyll in non-biological environments. In contrast, BCDs maintained relatively stable emission, highlighting the superior stability of graphitic carbon core-based fluorescence (Figure S3a &b). Based on these findings, a schematic representation of the synthesis and transition pathway is proposed in Fig. 3 f. Fig. 3 g illustrates the temperature- and time-dependent transformation process. At 150 °C, CDs preserve chlorophyll content, leading to red fluorescence with higher QY due to extended conjugation. Intermediate temperatures (∼200 °C) promote partial graphitization, while complete carbonization at 220 °C yields BCDs emitting stable blue light with reduced QY. Structural assessments confirmed that RCDs exhibited higher nitrogen content and lower graphitization (ID/IG = 1.05), whereas BCDs demonstrated greater graphitic order (ID/IG = 0.92). Over long-term storage, BCDs retained their emission intensity better than RCDs, reinforcing their structural and fluorescence stability [59]. These comprehensive results confirm that the CDs synthesized from M.P possess excellent photostability, satisfactory quantum efficiency, and long fluorescence lifetimes making them ideal candidates for diverse applications in sensing, lighting systems, and light-converting films (LCFs).

PVA is selected as the base film-forming material due to its affordability, high transparency, flexibility, excellent mechanical strength, non-toxic nature, and biocompatibility [60]. The inclusion of ascorbic acid (AA) along with PVA further enhanced the photostability of the incorporated CDs. Therefore, PVA served as the host matrix for RCDs in this composite system. In contrast, CA is utilized as the matrix for incorporating BCDs because of its low cost, good light transparency, minimal toxicity, and superior compatibility with biological systems. However, the acetyl groups in CA reduce the density of hydrogen bonding among cellulose chains, consequently lowering its mechanical toughness [61].

To overcome this limitation, a novel three-layered, sandwich-type light-converting agricultural film, denoted as R/B-CDs@PVA, is fabricated by integrating the advantages of both PVA and CA. Initially, RCDs are dispersed into the PVA matrix along with AA to form R-CDs@PVA films. Subsequently, BCDs are blended into the CA matrix to produce a coating solution. This solution is then applied over the R-CDs@PVA films using a dip-coating method, resulting in R/B-CDs@PVA composite films with a multilayer configuration. Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) is used to examine the surface morphology and cross-sectional structure of the composite films ( Fig. 4 a–d). The SEM images revealed that both R-CDs@PVA and R/B-CDs@PVA films maintained smooth, intact surfaces without visible cracks or voids. This integrity can be attributed to the excellent compatibility between RCDs, the PVA matrix, BCDs, and the CA coating layer. Even at higher BCDs loading, a homogeneous distribution is achieved, improving the functional performance of the films. When considered alongside FT-IR and XRD results, it is evident that strong hydrogen bonding interactions are formed between the PVA chains and the RCDs.

XRD patterns of the composite films are presented in Fig. 4 e. The diffraction peaks at 2θ values around 19.60° and 40.10° correspond to the semi-crystalline characteristics of PVA. The primary peaks seen in Fig. 2 j relate to CDs’ functional groups. Peaks in the crystalline regions primarily result from intermolecular hydrogen bonding among PVA chains [62]. Notably, no new crystalline peaks emerged after the incorporation of CDs, although slight shifts in peak positions are observed, in line with prior literature [63]. This implies that while CDs are uniformly dispersed in the PVA matrix, they do not disturb the crystalline structure. Their interaction with the PVA is likely due to hydrogen bonding rather than acting as nucleating agents [64]. FTIR spectra of R-CDs@PVA and R/B-CDs@PVA composite films (Fig. 4 f) further confirmed these interactions. Broad absorption bands between 3000 and 3500 cm−1 are associated with –OH stretching due to hydrogen bonds between PVA and CDs. The absorption peak at 2879 cm−1 corresponds to asymmetric stretching of CH2 groups. A new peak near 1660 cm−1 indicates the CO stretching vibration, while the bands around 940 cm−1 and 840 cm−1 correspond to C–N and C–C/CH2 stretching modes, respectively [65]. The plentiful –OH groups in PVA contribute to a rigid network structure by forming hydrogen bonds with –NH2 groups present on the RCD surface [66]. These strong hydrogen bonds limit polymer-solute interactions, reduce oxygen diffusion, and suppress collisional quenching processes, thereby enhancing fluorescence intensity and stability [67].

Thermal properties of the composite films are assessed through thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), with the results illustrated in Figure S4. The initial degradation observed between 30–150 °C is mainly attributed to moisture loss. A second degradation stage between 150–360 °C is linked to the breakdown of the polymer backbone and thermal decomposition of PVA. Notably, as BCDs content decreased, the thermal decomposition rate also slowed, suggesting enhanced thermal resistance due to BCD incorporation. The third degradation phase, occurring between 360–450 °C, likely corresponds to the breakdown of carbonaceous structures and byproducts of PVA decomposition [68]. These results confirm that the inclusion of CDs improves the thermal stability of the films. Similar results have been reported in earlier studies using CD composites. For instance, Zhao et al. prepared banana-based CD/PVA films and found that CDs enhance thermal stability [69].

To explore the luminescent behavior and potential multifunctional applications of composite films, a one-step fabrication strategy is initially employed. In this approach, PVA is used as the matrix, and two different types of CDs (BCDs and RCDs) are directly mixed in varying proportions (1–10 wt%) and cast into films ( Fig. 5 a). R-CDs@PVA showed intense red emission, while adding 1 wt% BCDs resulted in magenta emission. At 5 wt% BCDs, a violet emission is observed, and increasing to 10 wt% yielded strong blue fluorescence, confirming effective spectral modulation. This direct blending method offered a simplified and time-efficient route to composite formation, requiring no intermediate processing steps (Fig. 5 b). Despite the convenience of the one-step process, the resulting BCDs/PVA films exhibited relatively lower photoluminescent intensity, particularly at higher loading percentages. This reduced emission intensity is attributed to uncontrolled dispersion and possible self-quenching effects arising from close packing of the CDs within the PVA matrix. To overcome these limitations, a two-step fabrication process is subsequently adopted. In this method, CA dissolved in THF served as an alternative polymer matrix, providing a more tunable environment for CD dispersion. The RCDs and BCDs are introduced sequentially, allowing for improved control over their spatial distribution and minimizing undesirable interactions. This stepwise strategy facilitated better emission stability and enhanced color-tunability in the resulting films. Under ambient lighting conditions, the films exhibited a light purple hue that became more pronounced with increasing BCD content, while retaining excellent transparency (Fig. 5 c). When exposed to UV light (Fig. 5 d), the films demonstrated tunable fluorescence, with emission characteristics varying according to the concentration of BCDs. Fig. 5 e illustrates the transparency of the films, with images of flowers beneath the films remaining clearly visible demonstrating minimal light obstruction by the films. This indicates that the films can modulate fluorescence without sacrificing transparency, making them suitable for applications like agricultural lighting [70]. Transmittance spectra (Fig. 5 d) reveal broad absorption in the UV region, while maintaining transmittance above 60 % in the visible and infrared regions. Emission spectra (Fig. 5 e) mirrored those of RCDs and BCDs, and overlapped well with chlorophyll a and b absorption bands (Figure S5), highlighting their suitability for photosynthesis enhancement. Increasing BCD concentration gradually intensified blue emission, allowing for tunable blue-to-red light ratios, which are advantageous for supporting the specific light requirements of different crops [71].

Mechanical performance of the films is evaluated through tensile strength measurements ( Fig. 6 a), indicating robust mechanical stability of the R/B(5 wt%)-CDs@PVA composites. Testing using a universal testing machine (Fig. 6 b) showed that mixing BCDs with CA increased tensile strength from 67.14 MPa to 76.85 MPa. However, elongation at break declined from 38.55 % to 18.05 %, likely due to increased spacing between CA chains and uneven stress distribution [72]. Fig. 6 c demonstrates film flexibility, while water contact angle (WCA) analysis (Fig. 6 d) revealed improved hydrophobicity after BCD/CA coating ( Table 1). This is likely due to interactions between CDs and PVA surface groups, which hinder water droplet adhesion [49]. The film’s self-cleaning behaviour is illustrated schematically in Fig. 6 e, and practically shown in Fig. 6 f, where dust particles adhered to the surface are washed away by rolling water droplets, indicating effective self-cleaning action [73]. R/B(5 wt%)-CDs@PVA films are thus selected for extended testing.

Their overall stability is assessed under various treatments including mechanical stress, deformation, pH variation, thermal conditions, and exposure to bleaching agents ( Fig. 7). Tensile testing (Figure S6a) confirmed their durability. Under compressive loading, luminescence intensity declined as pressure increased (Fig. 7 a), while stretching tests (Fig. 7 b and S6b) showed a slight decrease in emission intensity with increasing elongation, suggesting resilience under mechanical stress. Photostability under sunlight exposure over 120 days (Fig. 7 c and S6c) showed a fluorescence retention of over 80.4 %, indicating excellent durability. Further, continuous UV irradiation (36 mW/cm2) for 240 min retained 93 % of initial PL intensity (Fig. 7 d and S6d). pH tests demonstrated stability across different conditions (Fig. 7 e and S6e). Exposure to bleaching agents like calcium hypochlorite for 20 days showed gradual fluorescence loss (Fig. 7 f and S6f), simulating oxidative stress and assessing chemical resistance [74]. Temperature stability tests at −25 °C and 100 °C demonstrated good retention of fluorescence intensity after 25 days (Fig. 7 g and S6g), with over 84 % of emission preserved at 100 °C (Fig. 7 h and S6h). Water immersion tests showed that films retained shape for over three days, with minimal mass loss, and fluorescence remained at 86 % after 20 days (Fig. 7 i and S6i), affirming excellent water resistance. For environmental degradation analysis, films are buried in moist soil, where they degraded into fragments over time (Figure S7), showcasing outstanding biodegradability for agricultural applications [75,76].

Building upon the above findings, plant-growth LED systems are developed using 395 nm emission chips combined with R/B(1, 5 and 10 wt%) CDs@PVA composite films, as illustrated in Fig. 8 a and Figure S8. These LEDs are designed to emit both blue and red wavelengths by varying the proportion of BCDs in the composite films (Fig. 8 b). As depicted in the EL spectra (Fig. 8 a and S8), the fabricated LED devices exhibited prominent red and blue emission peaks, and the intensity of the emitted light varied systematically with the change in BCDs concentration. The emission spectra displayed a gradual shift in color output, aligning with the variation in the mass ratio of the BCDs. A comparable approach is previously reported by Y. Xie et. al [77], who also fabricated plant-growth LEDs by tuning the ratio of RCDs and BCDs. In our study, however, we specifically utilized R/B-CDs@PVA composite films with variable BCD concentrations to tailor the spectral output for plant cultivation purposes. Although PVA-CD-based composite films have previously been explored in the context of white-light emitting diodes (WLEDs), limited research has focused on their application in agricultural LEDs for promoting plant development [78,79]. The visual appearance of the constructed LED devices is provided in the inset of Fig. 8 a. As the BCD content increased, the chromaticity coordinates on the CIE 1931 diagram shifted notably from red (0.589, 0.266) to near-white (0.292, 0.217) and ultimately to the blue region (0.205, 0.222), as shown in Fig. 8 b. These spectral transitions confirm that the composite films successfully modulate light emission across the visible range, making it possible to design LEDs with customizable light spectra based on plant-specific requirements. Consequently, LEDs with different red-to-blue emission ratios can fulfil diverse lighting needs for various crop species at different growth stages.

Additional photometric parameters such as luminous efficacy and CRI are also evaluated and are summarized in Table 2. Furthermore, the EL spectra and the variation of CIE chromaticity coordinates of the LED devices fabricated with R/B(5 wt%) CDs@PVA films under different driving currents (100–500 mA) are shown in Fig. 8 c. With increasing current from 100 to 500 mA, a slight shift toward the blue region is observed in the chromaticity coordinates from (0.330, 0.256) to (0.301, 0.221), as shown in Fig. 8 d. These observations indicate that the light intensity and color output can be further fine-tuned by adjusting the operating current, offering additional flexibility for agricultural lighting. Such controllability demonstrates that these LEDs are more advanced than conventional plant growth lamps and can provide precise lighting conditions tailored to specific agricultural environments and crop requirements [80,81]. To verify the effectiveness of these newly developed LEDs, their performance in indoor plant cultivation is evaluated, demonstrating the potential of R/B(5 wt%) CDs@PVA composite film-based LEDs in promoting efficient plant growth.

Plant lighting and film mulching play a crucial role in greenhouse cultivation for producing high-quality, pollution-free vegetables. LEDs offer advantages such as high brightness, low power consumption, and long lifespan, making them the primary artificial light source for indoor plant growth. LCFs can significantly enhance crop yield by improving light utilization efficiency. This study focuses on developing LCFs and LED devices for agricultural applications. Photosynthetic pigments strongly absorb blue and red light; thus, CDs that emit these two colors are ideal for plant lighting and light conversion films. For the first time, our group prepared BCDs and RCDs, dispersing them in PVA and CA matrices to create a transparent, uniform, and dense daylight conversion film. This film retained the CDs' inherent optical properties, effectively converting UV light into blue and red light, making it a promising candidate for agricultural light conversion applications. Furthermore, we developed the core-shell structured R/B(5 wt%) CDs@PVA composite film, which also converted UV to blue and red light while maintaining visible light transmittance and transparency.

Most CDs emit only blue fluorescence, while crops require both blue and red light for optimal growth. Few CDs emit both colours due to their photoluminescence (PL) mechanism. While excitation-dependent CDs can emit multiple colours, they may include green light, which is less useful for plants. Since only emissions within chlorophyll’s strong absorption range are beneficial for plant growth, researchers have combined red- and blue-emitting CDs within matrices to create nanocomposites capable of simultaneous red and blue light emission. These composites are intended for use in plant LCFs or LED-based plant lighting to enhance photosynthesis. Studies indicate that red light promotes radial growth, while blue light stimulates lateral extension [82,83]. The LED device developed in this study improved photosynthesis, chlorophyll content, nutrient accumulation, and total biomass in tomato plants. Additionally, RCDs can convert UV-A light into far-red emission (625–800 nm), which is directly absorbed by chloroplasts, accelerating electron transfer from PS II to PS I, increasing ATP production, and enhancing the fresh and dry weights of tomato plant. Similarly, Wang et al., [84] prepared CDs using the same precursor and method, which absorbed UV light and emitted red light, promoting photosynthesis in Nicotiana benthamiana. These CDs accelerated electron transfer in the photosystem, improved photosynthesis, and promoted plant growth.

Indoor experiments ( Fig. 9 a) showed that after 10 days, tomato plants grown under LEDs and LCFs exhibited greater lushness and vigor compared to those grown under sunlight. Plant height assessments indicated that tomatoes grown under LEDs are taller than those grown under sunlight or LCFs (Fig. 9 b). Additionally, light supplementation increased stem diameter (Fig. 9 c), and dry and fresh root weights significantly improved under LEDs compared to LCFs and sunlight (Fig. 9 d). Similarly, the fresh weight of shoots significantly increased under LED lighting, as shown in Table 3. Tomato plants grown under LEDs exhibited 1.5 times increase in fresh weight and 1.42 times increase in dry weight compared to those under sunlight. Leaf area also significantly expanded under LED lighting (Fig. 9 f). Furthermore, red and far-red light enhanced chlorophyll accumulation, with LED-grown tomatoes exhibiting a 16.8 % increase in chlorophyll a content and a 7.5 % increase in chlorophyll b content compared to the sunlight group (Fig. 9 g).

These findings suggest that LCFs improve plant growth by enhancing solar energy utilization, but LEDs are more effective due to their concentrated and stable light source, ensuring efficient energy transfer to chlorophyll for photosynthesis. Additionally, the directional emission of LEDs reduces light loss and enhances penetration into plant tissues, leading to more efficient chlorophyll excitation, improved photosynthesis, and enhanced plant growth [85].

The responsiveness of both RCDs and BCDs towards Res is systematically analysed to assess their sensing capabilities. RCDs demonstrated negligible sensitivity to Res, as their fluorescence emission spectra showed minimal change across a wide range of Res concentrations, as illustrated in Figure S9. Even with the incremental addition of Res, the fluorescence intensity of RCDs remained virtually unchanged, a result that is visually confirmed under UV illumination (inset of Figure S9), where the fluorescence color of RCDs is unaltered. Conversely, BCDs exhibited a pronounced quenching effect in the presence of Res, suggesting a specific and sensitive interaction mechanism. Upon gradual addition of Res to BCDs, a clear and significant decrease in fluorescence intensity is observed ( Figs. 10 a and 10 b), indicating effective and concentration-dependent detection. This quenching behavior confirmed the high sensitivity of BCDs toward Res. The fluorescence intensity decreased progressively with increasing Res concentrations (ranging from 0–10 mg/mL), and a well-defined linear correlation is established between the fluorescence response and Res concentration (Fig. 10 c). The linear regression analysis produced a strong correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.99), as shown in Fig. 10 d, highlighting the reliability of the detection method. The detection limit (LOD) is determined to be 7.76 mg/mL, calculated using the standard equation LOD = 3σ/k, where σ denotes the standard deviation of fluorescence intensities from 13 blank samples, and k represents the slope of the calibration curve [86]. The selectivity of BCDs for Res is further assessed in the presence of various potentially interfering substances, including a range of metal ions (Al3+, Ca2+, Cu2+, K+, Zn2+, Na+, Fe2+, Mg2+, F-, Fe3+, Cr3+, Ni2+, NO3 -, CO3 2-, HCO3 -, SO4 2-, and PO4 3-), biomolecules (Cys, His, and Arg), common food components (Mal, Glu, Vitamin C, Vitamin E, Ant, Kae, GA, Rut, CA, Pte, SI, and Pic), and pesticide residues (Diu and Mon). As presented in Figures S10a and S10b, these substances caused no notable interference with the fluorescence intensity of BCDs. However, the addition of Res consistently led to a substantial decline in fluorescence emission, indicating the excellent selectivity of BCDs for detecting Res even amidst complex backgrounds (Figure S10c).

To understand the underlying mechanism of interaction between BCDs and Res, fluorescence lifetime measurements are conducted in the absence and presence of Res. The results showed that the fluorescence lifetime of BCDs remained nearly constant despite Res addition, thus eliminating the possibility of dynamic quenching or FRET as contributing mechanisms (Fig. 10 e) [87]. To further investigate, UV–visible absorption spectra of BCDs are recorded before and after Res addition. A noticeable red shift in the absorption peak around 275 nm is observed upon Res introduction (Fig. 10 f), suggesting the occurrence of static quenching. XPS analysis provided additional insight into the interaction sites on BCDs. The N 1 s spectrum (Figure S1d) identified the presence of pyrrolic nitrogen (399.4 eV) and pyrazinic nitrogen (399.8 eV), which can form hydrogen bonds with the hydroxyl groups of Res molecules [88]. Moreover, π–π stacking interactions between the aromatic rings of Res and the conjugated structures of BCDs likely contributed to the red shift in the absorption spectra, reinforcing the static quenching hypothesis. The presence of multiple functional groups on the surface of BCDs provided numerous binding sites for Res, facilitating strong molecular interactions. Additionally, partial spectral overlap between the UV absorption band of Res and the excitation spectrum of BCDs (Fig. 10 g) implied the involvement of the inner filter effect (IFE) in the quenching mechanism [89]. FTIR analysis further substantiated the interaction, with shifts in characteristic absorption bands such as O–H stretching (∼3300 cm−1) and CO stretching (∼1650 cm−1) observed after Res is introduced to BCDs (Fig. 10 h). These spectral modifications confirmed the chemical interaction between BCDs and Res molecules, consistent with the fluorescence quenching behaviour (Fig. 10 i) [90]. In summary, the fluorescence quenching of BCDs upon Res addition can be attributed to a combined mechanism involving static quenching and the inner filter effect. This dual-mode interaction allows for a highly sensitive and selective detection system for Res, offering a promising approach for fluorescence-based biosensing applications.

In summary, a sustainable and straightforward solvothermal method is successfully developed for synthesizing multicolour CDs using M.P leaves as the sole carbon source. By fine-tuning the reaction parameters, the proportion between the chlorophyll-based red luminescent centres and the carbon-core-based blue luminescent centres is modulated, enabling controlled fluorescence emissions ranging from red to near-white and eventually to blue. The synthesized RCDs and BCDs are characterized using XRD, Raman spectroscopy, SEM, TEM, XPS, FTIR, and fluorescence spectroscopy. The RCDs displayed a bright red emission at 655 nm, while the BCDs emitted blue light at 480 nm, with quantum yields of 15.63 % and 12.23 %, respectively. HR-TEM analysis revealed that the average sizes of RCDs and BCDs are ∼ 6.71 nm and 6.46 nm, with an interlayer spacing of 0.21 nm (100). Furthermore, the BCDs exhibited high sensitivity and selectivity towards Res, showing a linear detection range of 0–10 mg/mL and a limit of detection (LOD) of 7.76 mg/mL. A biodegradable, sandwich-type LCF is fabricated by incorporating R/B(5 wt%)-CDs into a PVA matrix, which efficiently converted ultraviolet radiation into red and blue wavelengths that are readily absorbed by chlorophyll. The dual-emission LCFs and LEDs notably boosted photosynthetic activity and biomass accumulation in tomato plants, resulting in 1.5 times increase in fresh weight and 1.42 times increase in dry weight. Moreover, the use of biodegradable polymers as substrates offers an environmentally friendly alternative, mitigating ecological impact and supporting green agricultural development. In addition to agricultural benefits, the BCDs demonstrated excellent sensing performance for Res detection, with fluorescence quenching attributed to both inner filter effect (IFE) and static quenching mechanisms. The BCDs showed remarkable sensitivity and selectivity, highlighting their suitability as a reliable fluorescence-based sensing platform. Overall, this research establishes a versatile foundation for eco-friendly technologies in agriculture and opens new pathways for further exploration in light-conversion systems and plant-growth enhancement strategies.
